mappings.rationaleAI: Keynesian theory of effective demand contrasted with classical law of markets (A.1.a), Keynesian theory of unemployment (A.1.b), founding of the International Monetary Fund as an international monetary institution (B.2), and relevance of Keynesian economics during financial crises as countercyclical policy (C.3).

Individual and firm-level economic decision-making contrasting with macroeconomic unemployment and inflation concerns (A.1.b), and supply dynamics and market structures contributing to the circular flow of income and expenditure (A.2).

Macroeconomic measures including gross domestic product (A.2), resource allocation and market mechanisms affecting aggregate output (A), and economic development analysis contributing to understanding fluctuations in national income (A.3).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John O. S. Wilson, John Goddard
mappings.rationaleAI: Central bank monetary policy directly relevant to exchange rates and adjustment mechanisms (B.1, B.1.a), the 2008 financial crisis and sovereign debt crises as problems of international financial disequilibrium (B.2), and monetary policy as countercyclical fiscal and monetary policy (C.3).

International trade governance through the WTO affecting foreign exchange and balance of payments (B.1), the Bretton Woods system and IMF relationship to trade as international monetary institutions (B.2), most favoured nation status and comparative advantage as mechanisms affecting international equilibrium (B), and balance of payments issues (B.2.a).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Great Depression as a catastrophic business cycle illustrating cyclical theory (C, C.1), Keynesian economics as countercyclical fiscal policy response (C.3), the collapse of financial markets as fluctuation in national income (A.3), and the contrast between laissez-faire and interventionist approaches (A.1.a).

Asset valuation, risk management, and pricing of financial contracts in financial markets (B.1), arbitrage pricing theory relating to equilibrating movements (B.1.a), and hedging and speculation as disequilibrating movements (B.1.b).

The Great Depression and the 2007-8 financial crisis as business cycles (C, C.1), globalization of capital beyond nation-states as international financial dynamics (A.3), and economic crises and nationalism as aftermath of major upheavals (B.2.b).

Transnational economic flows affecting foreign exchange markets and international equilibrium (B.1), global trade and migration as factors in balance of payments (B), and international cooperation challenges relating to monetary and financial institutions (B.2).
The Wealth of Nations establishing foundational concepts of free trade and competition, relevant to the classical law of markets and market-based approaches to national income (A.1.a).

Analysis of global economic development illustrating fluctuations in national income across countries (A.3), and the role of institutional frameworks and government policy as international cooperation mechanisms (B.2).

Deregulation and free market policies as methods for adjusting to economic disequilibrium (B.1.d), and the 2008 financial crisis challenging neoliberal assumptions as a critical business cycle event requiring countercyclical response (C.3).
GDP, GNP, and purchasing power parity metrics as analyses of national income (A.3), and the International Monetary Fund as an international monetary institution addressing development (B.2).
Tax policy as a fiscal instrument for countercyclical monetary and fiscal policy affecting aggregate economic activity (C.3).

Marx's critique of political economy offering an alternative to both classical and Keynesian theories of markets and employment, relevant to contrasting theoretical approaches to national income (A.1.a).
